# **Spark Tuning: Rightsizing Executors**

Status: *Approved*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:chen.y@salesforce.com)   Created: Jun 17, 2024  Last Updated: Aug 29, 2024

# Goal

The goal of this document is to focus on tuning Spark jobs to capture cost savings without risking reliability by following the best practices for spark.executore.cores and spark.executor.memory. 

**Why is this important for job owners to right-sze the executors?** Because after you tune your job to use the appropriately sized containers (drivers and executors), not only is your job cheaper to run, it will run faster and more reliably. If you are able to reduce the executor size by 50%, your job will run more than 2x faster under the same cluster environment. This is because of two reasons: (1) you can fit into two containers instead of one for the same cluster available resources, and (2) the scheduler may find more resources that’s not available for larger containers because it is easier to find available slots for smaller resource requests, and (3) with FairScheduler, which focus on fairness, the job asking for less resources are prioritized. 

# Right-sizing Executors

## Tune spark.executor.cores, spark.executor.memory

**The following process focuses on reducing the memory-to-cpu ratio for cost savings. If you encounter a failed job with OOM on the executor, the process is the same, except that you will be increasing the memory-to-cpu ratio, while still following the best practices of setting spark.executore.cores to 4\.**

There is a known technical debt in our infrastructure that sets the spark.executor.cores=1, and also enforced by a corresponding YARN setting. As a result, all Spark jobs are configured with spark.executor.cores=1. This setting is not recommended as it will generate significant resource waste. I’ve reconfigured the YARN enforcement ([PR](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/pull/5934) in Feb, 2024\) to the best practice of 4, which enables the users to increase the spark.executor.core up to the new limit of 4\. The job owners need to override this value when tuning the jobs.

To check if your job needs tuning, open up the Spark UI of a recent run, then click on the “Environment” tab on the top of the page, and then search for “spark.executor.cores”. If you see the value as 1, it means that there is a good tuning opportunity to improve performance and reduce cost.

![][image1]

What does this spark.executor.cores mean? It means that each Spark executor is assigned with 1 CPU core, and it also means that when it is running tasks, it can only run one task at a time. This is an extreme setting that can potentially generate large waste. For example, if a task is more IO bound, spending 50% of the time doing IO, then 50% of the CPU core is wasted because the task is blocked for IO. There is a consensus to set this value to 4 or 5 to achieve a good balance between resource efficiency and parallelism. 

As a thought experiment, if a job is originally configured with spark.executor.cores=1, spark.executor.memory=4G, it means that each executor can only run one task at a time, and it can use 4G heap memory. It would fail if it uses more than 4G memory. If any task in a stage uses more than 4G memory, the application will fail. Now consider we change the configuration to setting spark.executor.cores=4, and spark.executor.memory=16G, we essentially make the executor size 4x as large, with the same memory-to-cpu ratio. Each such executor can run 4 tasks in parallel. However, with more parallelism, the executors as a whole are less likely to be blocked by IO and less likely to waste the more expensive resources (CPU and memory) because of slower IO. Besides more parallelism, the larger executor tolerates OOM much better. This is because the heap memory of 16G is now the new limit for a total of 4 tasks. If a few of these tasks use more than 4G, as long as the total usage is still less than 16G, the job will not fail. Even if the executor may fail occasionally because the total memory goes over the limit of 16G, when the tasks are retried, they may be retried on different executors. An interesting scenario is that sometimes data skew can cause just a few tasks to consume large amounts of memory. They may fail initially with max parallelism, but when they are being retried, all other short tasks have finished, and these tasks may be assigned to executors with no other tasks running, and as a result they can consume the full 16G memory. Another reason the larger executor is more resource efficient lies in the fact that many jobs use broadcast. For example, when one table used in a join operation is small enough, Spark will broadcast the table to all executors which can significantly speed up the job. Such broadcast values will consume memory resources, but only on the executor level. That is, for a larger executor with spark.executor.cores=4, all 4 tasks share the same copy of memory-intensive broadcast, which is more efficient than keeping one copy of the broadcast per one task. 

In conclusion, **larger executors with the same memory-to-cpu ratio are more reliable than smaller executors, which implies cost saving opportunities by reducing the memory-to-cpu ratio, to achieve the goal of increased reliability, performance, and reduced cost at the same time.**

To actually execute this optimization step, we follow the following general framework:

1. Confirm that there is a good opportunity through Spark UI.  
2. Increase spark.executor.cores from 1 to 4\. Based on metrics in Spark UI, decide the appropriate spark.executor.memory. The goal is to reduce the memory-to-cpu ratio.  
3. Pick a long-running instance, or a higher-risk run of the job from the past, and run it in staging-analytics-emr6 cluster to confirm that it still works.  
4. Deploy the change to production.

### *Confirm a good opportunity to increase spark.executor.cores*

The following screenshot comes from a real job running in the dma cluster on 2024-08-28 that needs to be tuned. [The live link for the Spark UI is here](https://emr-prod-dma-emr6-sparkhistory.tinyspeck.com/history/application_1724280707202_44801/1/environment/). The dag is metrics\_user\_8, the task is media\_playback.

1. In the Spark UI, find and navigate to the longest running stage in the job you are running. In this example, we first click on the “Stages” tab on the top of the page to navigate to the list of stages, then click on the “Duration” column in the “Completed Stages” section. You may need to click again, to ensure that the table is ordered by Duration decrementally. Then click on the link in the “Description” column to navigate to that specific stage. The following screenshot shows that the longest running stage has a duration of 12 minutes, with 34022 tasks in this stage.

![][image2]

2. Under the “Show Additional Metrics” section, click “Select All”. This allows you to see all metrics important for this stage.

![][image3]

3. Examine this all metrics table, check if there are memory spills, disk spills. The following is an example. What we are looking for is the “existence” of the memory spills and disk spills. If there are no spills, these two rows simply don’t show up in the table. The following is an example of a stage with 34022 tasks, with no spills. Having a small amount of spill is healthy, but no spills means the executor has enough memory that it never needs to spill, and this is a good candidate to reduce executor memory.

![][image4]

4. Scroll down on the same page, look for the section named “Aggregated Metrics by Executor”. It shows metrics aggregated by the executor, and you will see a table. There is a column named “Peak JVM Memory OnHeap/OffHeap”. This column shows the peak memory each executor used during this stage. Click on the column name on the table allows you to sort by this column, incremental or decrementally. We want to look for the executor that has used the max amount of memory in this stage, so you may click a couple of times. The following screenshot shows that for the stage, the most heap memory an executor ever consumed among all executors is about 1.8G heap. Note down this number somewhere.

![][image5]

5. Now, click on the “Environment” tab on the top of the Spark UI page, which shows all configurations for this job. Search for the string “spark.executor.memory” using browser search. The following screenshot shows that this job has configured each executor to use 12GB heap memory. This confirms that this job is wasting 85% of heap memory for the executors that used most heap. The wastage is even higher for other executors that use less than the max of 1.8G we noted from the previous step. At this point, we have the confidence that this job needs to be tuned immediately to reduce the wastage.  
   

![][image6]

6. In the same “Environment” page, search for “spark.executor.cores” and note down the number. The following screenshot shows that each executor is running with only 1 core, which is not the best practice. Now note down somewhere the following facts: this job has sized its executors to use 1 core and 12G heap memory each, but it used 1.8G heap memory at the peak time, wasting more than 85% of resources it requests considering many other executors never get to use 1.8G heap.  
   ![][image7]  
7. To build more confidence in the tuning and reduce risk, make sure to iterate the same process above for a couple of additional runs (especially the run with more data). Also, if the longest running few stages has similar duration, then it helps to check the other long running stages as well.

### *Right-sizing the containers based on the findings above*

We can go through the following steps to find the right sized executors.

1. Decide on a new heap size for the executor that gives enough head space in case there are day-to-day variations of data size. In this example, since the max usage is 1.8G, we may be confident that 3G is more than enough. One heuristic is that the heap size should not be 50% higher than the max usage. If the max usage is 10G, I think anywhere between 13G to 15G are safe guesses.   
2. Now also look at the spark.executor.cores. What we want is to see if there is a way to increase the spark.executor.cores to 4 while maintaining the same memory-to-CPU ratio. In this case, if we were to set vcores to 4 from the original value of 1, it means we should also multiply the heap size by 4\. Assuming we choose the 3G value from the first step, then we should set spark.executor.memory to 12G. The heuristics here is that when you use more cores, it has more tolerance for OOM, so if the multiplier is larger, I will have more confidence to choose the lower number in the range I determined in step \#1 above. For example, if in step one I would consider the range between 13G to 15G, but if the multiplier for vcores is 4, I will be more confident to choose the lower bound 13G.  
3. Update your job configs based on the new spark.executor.cores and spark.executor.memory. In this example, note that the original setting was (cores=1, memory=12G). The new config is (cores=4, memory=12G). Essentially we are increasing the number of cores per executor, without changing the heap memory, but each new executor can run 4 tasks in parallel instead of 1 task. This may mean this stage will run 4 times faster. Assuming the cluster has enough vcores resources, it means your job just increased parallelism by 4 times without using more memory. The good news is that all of our clusters have more available vcores than available memory.

### *Confirm the tuning worked*

After deploying the change, we want to confirm that the tuning has achieved the intended result. This including the following steps:

1. Confirm from Spark UI that the config changes (e.g.spark.executor.cores) that are in fact changed. This is because we have seen a few cases that the config changed were not applied in the right place in the source code.   
2. Confirm from Spark UI, “Summary Metrics for \_\_ Tasks” that all metrics still look healthy. Specifically, verify that after the change:  
   1. There are no GC pause concern  
   2. There are no significant spill  
3. Confirm from Spark UI, “Aggregated Metrics by Executor” section of the longest running stage, sorted by “Peak JVM Memory onHeap/offHeap” and the max value is still below the spark.executor.memory setting. If it is far below, we can continue the iteration by reducing spark.executor.memory.  
4. Confirm from Spark UI, “Tasks” section of the longest running stage, sort by “Errors” and inspect if there are any failing tasks caused by the tuning.  
5. Confirmed from Spark UI, in the “Event Timeline” section of the longest running stage, you can see the right number of parallel tasks running in parallel. Sometimes you need to increase the number of “Show \_\_ items in a page” to a larger number like 1000 to see a better visual. For example, the following screenshot shows 4 parallel tasks running at the same time for most executors.

![][image8]

# More Examples

* This is [an example case](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C8EDM9MGA/p1716920633561249) showing the incremental iterations on such tunings.  
* This is an [example](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1724803257496799?thread_ts=1724092217.163629&cid=CJFHE7AR3) of a job that wasted 90% of memory resources.
